coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical biosensing platform based on molecularly imprinted polymer reinforced by ZnO–graphene capped quantum dots for 6-mercaptopurine detection
coredata.dc:creator: Hatamluyi, Behnaz
Es'haghi, Zarrin
coredata.dc:description: A new quantum dot consisting of a ZnO core wrapped in a shell of graphene (ZnO@GQD) was synthesized and applied for the construction of an innovative electrochemical sensor for sensitive and selective determination of 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP). The sensor was fabricated based on the pencil graphite electrode (PGE) coated with a sol-gel binder reinforced with polypyrrole (PPy) based molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP), and ZnO@GQD core-shell nanoparticles with a homogeneous environment. The procedure was carried out through a facile one-step electropolymerization stage. The synthesized quantum dots were studied by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometry, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX). Electrochemical characterization of the sensor was performed by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). To clarify the oxidation mechanism of 6-MP, various voltammetry techniques were used. Electrochemical measurements using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method showed a wide linear relationship between 6-MP concentration and peak height within the range 0.01–50.0 μM and 50.0–700.0 μM with a lower detection limit (5.72 nM). The imprinted sensor was highly sensitive and was successful to detect 6-MP in real spiked samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 6-Mercaptopurine
Molecularly imprinted polymer
ZnO-graphene quantum dot core-shell nanoparticles
Sol-gel

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of molecularly imprinted polymer film modified PGE
Electrochemical behavior of 6-MP on polypyrrole/sol-gel/ZnO@GQDs/MIP PGE

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) FT-IR spectra of graphite, graphite oxide and ZnO@GQDs, B) XRD pattern of ZnO@GQDs, SEM images of (C) graphite oxide and (D) ZnO@GQDs and E) EDX image of ZnO@GQDs.

FE-SEM images of (A) bare PGE, (B) polypyrrole/sol-gel/ZnO@GQDs MIP PGE after removal of 6-MP, (C) side view of polypyrrole/sol-gel/ZnO@GQDs MIP modification at PGE. D) CVs of (a) bare PGE, (b) MIP PGE, (c) MIP PGE after removal of 6-MP, (d) MIP PGE after 180 s incubating in 5.0 μM 6-MP solution and (e) MIP PGE without ZnO@GQDs after removal of 6-MP. E) EIS of (a) bare PGE, (b) MIP PGE, (c) MIP PGE after removal of 6-MP, (d) MIP PGE after 180 s incubating in 5.0 μM 6-MP solution and (e) MIP PGE electrode without ZnO@GQDs after removal of 6-MP. Inset shows equivalent circuit model for impedance plots.

(A) CVs of 300 μM in PBS (pH 8.0) at polypyrrole/sol-gel/ZnO@GQDs/MIP PGE at different scan rate (30–240 mV s−1). (B) The linear relationship of the anodic peak current vs. square root of scan rate, (C) dependence of log IP vs. log v. (D) SWV curves of 100 μM 6-MP in 0.1 M PBS (pH 8.0) on polypyrrole/sol-gel/ZnO@GQDs/MIP PGE at different square wave frequency: from inner to outer are: 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70 HZ. (E) and (F) are relationships between the reduction peak current and square wave frequency, the peak potential and the natural logarithm of square wave frequency, respectively. The SWV parameters: potential scan range, 0.0 to +0.7 V; rest time, 2s; pulse amplitude, 0.05 V; frequency range, 5–70 Hz.

DPV curves of polypyrrole/sol-gel/ZnO@GQDs/MIP PGE in different concentrations of 6-MP (from bottom to top): 0.00, 0.01, 1.5, 5.0, 10.0, 20.0, 30.0, 50.0, 100.0, 250.0, 400.0, 550.0 and 700.0 μM. The insets show the calibration curve of 6-MP and each point of the calibration graph is the average of six replications.

(A) The evaluate selectivity of 6-MP (10.0 μM) in the presence of structurally similar drugs (a: 0.0, b: 10.0; c: 15.0 and d: 20.0 μM) and other interfering molecules (a: 0.0, e: 1000, f: 2500, g: 3500). (B) Selectivity of the imprinted and non-imprinted sensor for 6-MP and interferences.
Characteristics of 6-MP calibration plot using differential pulse voltammetry at polypyrrole/sol-gel/ZnO@GQDs/MIP PGE.

RSD for repeatability (%) a for 50.0 μM 6-MP
RSD for repeatability (%) a for 250.0 μM 6-MP
RSD for intra-day reproducibility (%) a for 5.0 μM 6-MP.27
RSD for intra-day reproducibility (%) a for 50.0 μM 6-MP
RSD for intra-day reproducibility (%) a for 250.0 μM 6-MP
RSD for inter-day reproducibility (%) a for 5.0 μM 6-MP.09
RSD for inter-day reproducibility (%) a for 50.0 μM 6-MP
RSD for inter-day reproducibility (%) a for 250.0 μM 6-MP

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: (1) Related to the low linear range.
(2) Related to the high linear range.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The results have been given average of six measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The results of 6-MP determination in real samples.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical biosensing platform based on molecularly imprinted polymer reinforced by ZnO–graphene capped quantum dots for 6-mercaptopurine detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Payame Noor University, 19395-4697 Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new quantum dot consisting of a ZnO core wrapped in a shell of graphene (ZnO@GQD) was synthesized and applied for the construction of an innovative electrochemical sensor for sensitive and selective determination of 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP). The sensor was fabricated based on the pencil graphite electrode (PGE) coated with a sol-gel binder reinforced with polypyrrole (PPy) based molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP), and ZnO@GQD core-shell nanoparticles with a homogeneous environment. The procedure was carried out through a facile one-step electropolymerization stage. The synthesized quantum dots were studied by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometry, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX). Electrochemical characterization of the sensor was performed by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). To clarify the oxidation mechanism of 6-MP, various voltammetry techniques were used. Electrochemical measurements using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method showed a wide linear relationship between 6-MP concentration and peak height within the range 0.01–50.0 μM and 50.0–700.0 μM with a lower detection limit (5.72 nM). The imprinted sensor was highly sensitive and was successful to detect 6-MP in real spiked samples.

Steps for doing experimental modification of pretreated PGEs.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: 6-mercaptopurine (C5H4N4S, MPH) is an established chemotherapeutic agent, widely utilized for the treatment of various types of cancer and inflammatory bowel diseases such as ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease [1,2]. This drug can inhibit purine metabolism and expansion of the tumor cells. But, there are some serious side effects associated with 6-MP and this limit its applications [3]. Previous studies have proven that the 6-MP plasma concentration is low and highly variable with individual differences and its response to maintenance therapy is also different, for this reason, 35% of acute lymphoblastic leukemia patients relapsed after treatment [4–6]. So, development of advanced and perspective analytical methods for highly sensitive control of 6-MP concentrations in biological samples are still desired.

Numerous techniques, such as Chemi Luminescence (CL) [6,7], High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) [8,9], Raman spectroscopy [10], and electrochemical methods [11,12] have been reported for the determination of 6-MP. CL methods have poor selectivity despite their high sensitivity. Although chromatography techniques offer excellent advantages in trace level analysis. But these methods need complicated pretreatment due to its higher requirements for the extraction and purification of the sample. Raman analyses also suffer from some of analogous disadvantages as chromatography. Therefore, among the reported analytical techniques, electrochemical technique is more powerful because of its advantages of low expense, rapidity, simple operation, easy monitoring and possibility of miniaturization [13]. These approaches, particularly electrochemical molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) based sensors, were used as a preeminent analytical tools because of their high binding selectivity, easy preparation, high efficiency, and good stability [14]. Molecular imprinting is one of the best approaches for making recognition patterns of molecules in different sizes [15]. In the MIP method by adding template molecules in the starting material, recognition cavities would be produced in the polymer film and with elimination of templates from the polymer matrix, nano-scale cites similar to the template molecules were generated. So, MIPs with high selectivity and performance are widely utilized as a chemical/biological sensing agent for electrochemical sensors.

Sol-gel imprinting technique is a favorable method to increase the MIPs efficiency [16]. Different from MIP possess, sol-gel based MIP methods are competitive in the easy construction procedure, the stability of chemical and thermal, physical rigidity and matrix porosity because of highly cross-linked structure [17]. However, the low electrical conductivity and slow diffusion kinetics of the sol-gel coatings is certainly a main disadvantage [18,19]. One powerful solution to these problems is the usage of conductive polymers and nanomaterials [20]. Among the various conductive polymers, polypyrrole is a superior choice which is ascribed to its considerable electrical conductivity, good biocompatibility, and easy polymerization [21]. The electropolymerization could produce a robust sensing film with excellent junction to electrode surface and perform a direct effective interaction between the MIPs layer and the transducer.

Today, quantum dots are widely used as very small semiconductor particles in nanoscale devices due to their unique properties such as high quantum yield, good chemical and photo-stability and size-tunable light emission [22,23].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are particular materials with a unique sp2 and sp3 hybrid structure. Because of their tunable properties, GQDs are of great interest [24–26]. In the comparison between carbon-based nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotube and graphene, GQDs have larger number of carboxyl groups, high surface-to-volume ratio, and high conductivity. The carboxylic moieties at GQDs edges improve water dispersibility and formation of complex with various compounds including inorganic/organic, polymeric, or biological compounds [27,28].

As is clear, one of the problems that limits the electrical properties of this valuable material is the lack of a band gap in graphene. To solve this problem, a hybrid nanocomposites combining of metal-based semiconductor nanoparticle with graphene was employed to realize the superior electrical conductivity and excellent catalytic activity in the construction of electrochemical sensors.

Zinc oxide (ZnO) is a significant semiconductor with a wide band gap and a large exciton binding energy, which has been used in solar cell, lasing, and detectors [29–31] and ZnO quantum dots (ZnO QDs) are even more attractive due to their interesting properties such as excellent catalytic capability, higher chemical stability, broad adsorption, and unique electronic properties [32]. On the other hand, in comparison with the traditional semiconductor QDs, ZnO QDs are biodegradable, nontoxic, relatively inexpensive, and biocompatible [33,34]. According to the characteristics of GQDs and ZnO QDs, ZnO–graphene quantum dots (ZnO@GQDs) were presented as excellent components for sensing devices because of their unique properties [35].

Here, we synthesized conductive quantum dots consisting of a ZnO core wrapped in a shell of graphene by a novel and simple technique. Considering the superiorities of MIP sensors, combination of ZnO@GQDs, conductive polymers, and sol-gel technology, the main purpose of the present work is to fabricate a MIP sensor with suitable sensitivity and selectivity to determine the 6-MP (Scheme 1 ). The experimental parameters that have an influence on MIP sensor efficiency were studied and optimized by electrochemical methods in detail. The developed sensor showed outstanding features such as high sensitivity and selectivity, durability, wide linear dynamic range, and excellent performance in the study of biological and pharmaceutical samples with satisfactory results. This work represents the first attempt of fabricating MIP sensor based on a conducting polymer, sol-gel, and ZnO@GQDs core–shell quantum dots for 6-MP sensing.
Detailed information on the reagents, equipment, synthesized ZnO@GQDs core–shell quantum dots method, as well as the experimental steps are provided in the Supplementary Materials.

The 6-MP imprinted sol solution was prepared by mixing 100 μL PTEOS, 100 μL TEOS, 1000 μL water, 1200 μL ethanol, 15 μL TFA and 6-MP (3.0 mM). As a result of stirring the mixture for 2 h at room temperature, a homogeneous sol was formed. Instantly following, 50 μL of pyrrole solution (25.0 mM), and 50 μL of 0.15% (%W/V) ZnO@GQDs solution were added to the prepared sol solution and it was sonicated for 10 min. Then, the 6-MP imprinted sol-gel film was deposited on the surface of pretreated PGE by CV runs (10 cycles between −0.8 and + 0.8 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1). After electrodeposition, the prepared polypyrrole/sol-gel/ZnO@GQDs MIP/PGE was dried for 2 h at room temperature. The 6-MP template molecules elimination was done by DPV scanning between +0.1 and + 0.8 V in PBS pH 8.0, for several times until no voltammetric signal was detected. As a control non-imprinted polypyrrole/sol-gel/ZnO@GQDs electrode was prepared by the same procedure, but without the presence of template molecules (6-MP).
Characterization of the ZnO@GQDs was confirmed by using FT-IR spectroscopy, XRD, EDX analysis and FE-SEM.

The FT-IR spectra of graphite, graphite oxide and ZnO@GQDs are shown in Fig. 1 A. Graphite oxide, exhibited vibrational bands at 1050 cm−1, 1716 cm−1 and 3232 cm−1, due to the presence of typical oxygen functional groups, COC, CO and OH respectively and the peak observed at 1620 cm−1 is attributed to CC stretching vibrations [36]. In the ZnO@GQDs spectrum, a new peak was observed at 420 cm−1 which is assigned to appearance of ZnO bonds and for that, the intensity of CO, CC, CO, and OH peaks reduced significantly. This results proves that the ZnO@GQDs core-shell nanoparticles were synthesized [37]. As well as Fig. 1B shows the XRD patterns of the ZnO@GQDs core–shell quantum dots. In this pattern the broad (002) and (100) diffraction peaks at 2θ ≈ 25.88° and 2θ = 43.58° respectively, are attributed to graphene and (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103) and (112) diffraction peaks at 2θ of 33.05°, 35.60°, 37.62°, 47.77°, 57.27°, 62.18° and 67.87° respectively, are corresponding to the standard ZnO bulk [38,39].

Morphological features of the ZnO@GQDs was investigated using FE-SEM images. Fig. 1C (a) and (b) show FE-SEM images of the graphite oxide and ZnO@GQDs respectively. ZnO@GQDs FE-SEM image displays the uniform spherical structure of nanoparticles. The smooth surface and quite uniform size distributions of ZnO@GQDs is also evident from this picture. The chemical composition of ZnO@GQDs core-shell quantum dots were further characterized by EDX (Fig. 1D). The observed peaks proved the presence of zinc and oxygen, along with carbon.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of molecularly imprinted polymer film modified PGE
ce:section.ce:para: In the imprinting procedure of 6-MP, Phenyltriethoxysilane (PTEOS) was used as functional monomer because of its phenyl group, which can interact with the 6-MP via π–π interactions [40] and the other functional monomer pyrrole was used to increase the stability of the MIP film. The 6-MP molecules have CS and NH groups which could be involved in hydrogen-bonding formation with the NH groups of pyrrole units [41]. Considering the good characteristics of tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), such as condensation through hydrogen bonds and ionic interactions and rapid rate of hydrolysis [42], it was used as cross-linker. Electrodeposition of sol-gel-MIP film on the PGE surface was carried out by using CV method. The CV curves obtained during the electro-polymerization process, in presence and absence of the template, at a scan rate of 50 mVs−1 between −0.8 V and +0.8 V are shown in Fig. S1 and detailed explanation about that are presented in the Supplementary Materials. Morphological images before and after polymerization are shown in Fig. 2 . It is obvious to see that the electrode surface was completely covered with the film when comparing it with unmodified PGE in Fig. 2B. The side image of the MIP modified layer on the electrode revealed that the coating thickness was 77.13 nm (Fig. 2C).

CV and EIS techniques were utilized to study the quality of the imprinted film. The inset of Fig. 2D demonstrated voltammograms of different electrodes using [Fe(CN)6]3−/[Fe(CN)6] 4− as probe. A pair of redox peaks of ferricyanide ions was observed on the bare electrode (curve a). However, there was no redox peak current after electro-polymerization of polypyrrole/sol-gel/ZnO@GQDs MIP, curve (b), without eluting of the template 6-MP, which indicates that the fabrication of a compact film with excellent shielding efficiency. When the template was removed from the polymer film, a considerable increase in the peak current was occurred (curve c). Furthermore, the peak current decreased once again (curve d), when the MIP PGE was incubated in 5.0 μM 6-MP solution for 180 s, suggesting that some cavities were recombined with 6-MP molecules to hamper the electron transfer of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− probe to the surface of the electrode. To study the effect of signal amplification by ZnO@GQDs, we organized polypyrrole/sol-gel MIP/PGE (without ZnO@GQDs) after removal of 6-MP at the same conditions. It was observed that when the electrode was modified by polypyrrole/sol-gel the redox signal decreased notably compared to polypyrrole/sol-gel/ZnO@GQDs MIP/PGE (curve e). The presence of ZnO@GQDs caused development in the electron transfer rate corresponding to the good conductivity of ZnO@GQDs core-shell nanoparticles.

Afterwards, the active surface area of the different electrodes was calculated according to the Randles – Sevcik equation (using 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-,4- and 0.1 M KCl) [43]. The average value of the electroactive surface area for template-free polypyrrole/sol-gel/ZnO@GQDs MIP/PGE was 0.721 ± 0.003 cm2, which was 1.6 times that of polypyrrole/sol-gel/ZnO@GQDs MIP/PGE with template (after rebinding of 6-MP molecules) (0.450 ± 0.005 cm2), 2.3 times that of polypyrrole/sol-gel/MIP/PGE without ZnO@GQDs (0.313 ± 0.002 cm2) and 3.1 times that of bare PGE (0.227 ± 0.005 cm2).

The stepwise construction process of the modified electrodes was also characterized by EIS technique. It was done with a polarization potential of 0.25 V and a frequency range between of 0.1 Hz–30000 Hz and at an amplitude of 10 mV. As shown in Fig. 2E, the results of the EIS characterization are in agreement with the results of the CV characterization.
DPV method was used as a sensitive and high-resolution method to investigate the effect of experimental factors on the peak current of 5.0 μM 6-MP at imprinted sensor.

In the process of electro-polymerization, two effective factors, the range of potentials and the number of scan cycles were optimized (Fig. S2). In the preparation of sol-gel, the dosages of (A) PTEOS: TEOS ratio, (B) water, (C) ethanol, (D) TFA, (E) Pyrrole, (F) 6-MP and (G) ZnO@GQDs for constructing MIP layer, were investigated via Taguchi L27 design of experiments [44] and ANOVA analysis was used to assess the quality and validity of the experimental design. The results displayed in Tables S1, S2, S3 and S4 as well as Fig. S3 reveal that the best combination is: 200 μL of silane monomers (100 μL of TEOS and 100 μL of PTEOS), 1000 μL water, 1200 μL ethanol, 15 μL TFA, 25 mM pyrrole, 3.0 mM 6-MP and 0.15% percent (% W/V) of ZnO@GQDs. The effect of accumulation time and the effect of background solution pH, were also investigated (Fig. S4 and Fig. S5). Detailed information of this studies is presented in the Supplementary Materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of 6-MP on polypyrrole/sol-gel/ZnO@GQDs/MIP PGE
ce:section.ce:para:
The effect of the scan rates from 30 to 240 mV s−1 on the analytical response was studied by CV (in the concentration of 300 μM 6-MP). As the scan rate increased, the anodic peak currents (Ipa) of 6-MP in PBS (pH 8.0) enhanced and shifted to more positive values (Fig. 3 A). This event could represent an irreversible reaction. As illustrated in Fig. 3B a linear dependency between peak current and square root of scan rate can be concluded by the regression equation of Ipa(μA) = 0.1282 v
1/2 (V s−1)1/2–0.0118 (R2 = 0.9950). As well as the plot of log IP vs. log v (Fig. 3C) showed slope values of 0.5795. This slope value is close to the theoretical value of 0.5, typical for a redox process controlled only by diffusion mass transport. These results clearly indicate a diffusion-controlled electro oxidative process [45]. Using the equation below, the number of electrons involved in the oxidation process was determined (1) [46]:
(1)
[
E P
−
E
p / 2
]
=
47.7
αn
a

In this equation EP/2, α and n represent the potential corresponding to IP/2, the transfer coefficient and the number of electrons transferred, respectively. It is usual that in an irreversible reaction for organic molecules, n is considered to be 0.5 [47]. Accordingly, the number of electrons transmitted in the electro oxidation of 6-MP was equal to 2.

The electrochemical behaviors of 100 μM 6-MP at polypyrrole/sol-gel/ZnO@GQDs/MIP PGE was also studied by the square wave voltammetry (SWV) (Fig. 3D). This study helped to better understand the mechanism of oxidation of the 6-MP and confirmed the accuracy of the obtained results in the two previous sections. Fig. 3E shows a linear increase in the peak current of 100 μM 6-MP with increase in square wave frequency (f) within the range of 5–70 s−1, with a linear equation of Ip(μA) = 1.1153f + 27.644 (r = 0.9971), as expected for the irreversible process [48]. Additionally, SWV theory was utilized to calculate the number of participating electrons in the redox process, using the following relationship [49]:
(2)
E P
= −
RT
αnF
lnf
Here, Ep, α, n and f signify the peak potential, the transfer coefficient, the number of participating electrons in the oxidation reaction, and the frequency, respectively; the other terms have their usual meaning. The slope obtained from the Ep vs. ln f plots (Fig. 3F) is −0.0275. By means of Eq. (2), a value equal to 0.934 is determined for αn. Hence, n is calculated as 1.868 (approximately equal to 2) if the value of α is assumed as equal to 0.5. These results are accordant with CV studies and pH effect studies as described in details above. The proposed mechanism is as shown in Fig. S6, which is in agreement with the previous accepted mechanism [50].

In the following, electroanalytical procedures including DPV and SWV were assessed for the quantitative analysis of 6-MP. It was illustrated that DPV was more sensitive than SWV for the quantitative determination of 6-MP.

Under the obtained optimized parameters, to test the practical application of proposed MIP sensor, 6-MP solutions in the concentration range of 0.01 μM–700 μM were used to create the calibration curves by DPV measurements. For this, after removal of template molecules, MIP electrode was immersed in different concentrations of 6-MP solution and the plot of peak current vs. 6-MP concentration was drawn up. According to the results, the presented polypyrrole/sol-gel/ZnO@GQDs/MIP PGE offers well-defined concentration dependence (Fig. 4 ). As is shown in Fig. 4, two linear ranges from 0.01 to 50.0 μM and 50.0–700.0 μM for the analyte are found with the linear regression equations of I (μA) = 0.9462 C 6-MP + 1.7951 (R2 = 0.9982) and I (μA) = 0.0490 C RTX + 47.1350 (R2 = 0.9971) respectively, here C is 6-MP concentration (μM) and, I is the anodic peak current (μA). The detection limit (DL) in the lower range region is estimated to be 5.72 nM based on 3 Sb/m.

The repeatability experiment of the MIP sensor was performed by determining the DPV responses of 5, 50 and 250 μM 6-MP in 0.1 M PBS (pH 8.0), and obtained RSDs (2.61%, 3.08% and 3.29% for six successive determinations) exhibited that the polypyrrole/sol-gel/ZnO@GQDs/MIP PGE was recoverable and reused. As well as six consecutive measurements were carried out based on the DPV signal by using six different electrodes to study reproducibility. The RSD% for 6 intraday experiments was 3.27% for 5 μM, 3.59% for 50 μM and 3.41% for 250 μM whereas it was calculated 4.09% for 5 μM, 4.15% for 50 μM and 4.17% for 250 μM in the 6 inter-day experiments. In addition, the lifetime of the designed MIP electrode was studied by measuring the DPV responses in 5 μM, 50 μM and 250 μM of 6-MP solutions every three days over the period of six weeks, and it was found that the sensor can retain 91.26%–88.63% of its initial response after 42 days' storage. The calibration curves details as well as the results of repeatability, reproducibility and stability studies are reported in Table 1 . These results revealed that the fabricated MIP sensor was robust and could be used for 6-MP analysis with acceptable repeatability, reproducibility, and stability.

To confirm the performance of the prepared MIP sensor, the specific recognition ability of polypyrrole/sol-gel/ZnO@GQDs/MIP PGE toward the 6-MP was evaluated by measuring of 10 μM 6-MP in the presence of the structurally similar analogues (6-thioguanine, allopurinol), co-existing interferes in the real samples (glucose, sucrose, ascorbic acid, uric acid, citric acid and tartaric acid) and several ions using DPV. The results indicated that the oxidation signal of 10 μM 6-MP were influenced less than 5% by 2 equiv. 6-thioguanine and allopurinol, 250 equiv. sucrose, glucose, ascorbic acid, 100 equiv. uric acid, 350 equiv. citric acid and tartaric acid, and 500 equiv. K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+,
NO
3 −
,
SO
4
2 −
and
Cl
−
. It was observed that the ions exhibit negligible influence at the peak current of 6-MP. The results of the interference study of other interfering compounds are graphically shown in Fig. 5 . Also, to more confirm the anti-interference capability of the sensor, the response of the MIP and NIP electrodes to the 6-MP in the presence of 10-fold excess of interfering species was investigated. As is evident in Fig. 5B, the current response of the MIP electrode for 6-MP is considerably higher than that of the other interfering compounds. The imprinting factor (IF) which is defined as the ratio of the IMIP to the INIP, was applied for evaluation of the selectivity of the prepared sensor. As shown in Fig. 5B, the IF value for 6-MP is 14.7 while for the other species it is in the range of 1.3–2.1. These observations clearly proved that polypyrrole/sol-gel/ZnO@GQDs/MIP PGE array has a satisfactory performance in selectivity.

The designed MIP sensor was applied to measure 6-MP concentration in pharmaceutical dosages and more complex human serum and urine samples to further validate its potential applications. The results of 6-MP determination in pharmaceutical formulation, human serum and urine samples with the relative recoveries of the spiked 6-MP are listed in Table 2 . As shown in Table 2, the recoveries are ranged from 96.23% to 104.28% with RSDs of 1.0–5.0% which confirms that the newly developed sensor does not suffer from any considerable matrix effect.

Table S5 compares the performance of the fabricated sensor with several previous literature procedures for 6- MP determination. According to this table it is obvious that, the polypyrrole/sol-gel/ZnO@GQDs/MIP PGE presents a wider linear dynamic range and a low LD which demonstrates that the fabricated MIP sensor could be applied as a promising candidate to determine the ultra–trace level of 6-MP in real samples.

This work describes the construction process of a novel MIP sensor for determination of 6-MP using ZnO@GQDs core shell quantum dots nanocomposite in a sol-gel molecularly imprinted polymer which is deposited on the pencil graphite electrode surface during a one-step polymerization. Ultrasensitive 6-MP detection was obtained due to facile electron transfer between the 6-MP selective recognition cavities, which readily captured 6-MP in a suitable configuration, and the superior properties of ZnO@GQDs core shell quantum dots nanocomposite. It is remarkable that the rapid response and easy removal of the template are the superior features of the fabricated sensor over the time consuming and hard washing conditions that usually reported in the literature for the MIPs. Overall, this sensor presented a simple and reliable method to detect 6-MP with wide potential applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thanks Payame Noor University for supporting of this research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2018.07.068.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The treatment of inflammatory bowel disease with 6-mercaptopurine or azathioprine
Clinical pharmacology and pharmacogenetics of thiopurines
6-Mercaptopurine (6-MP) induces cell cycle arrest and apoptosis of neural progenitor cells in the developing fetal rat brain
An efficient ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on metal-organic frameworks and quantum dots for highly selective detection of 6-mercaptopurine
Fabrication and evaluation of [Co(phen)2L]3+-modified DNA-MWCNT and SDS-MWCNT electrodes for electrochemical detection of 6-mercaptopurine
Sensitive chemiluminescence method for the determination of glutathione, L-cysteine and 6-mercaptopurine
